title: What to Do on the Sabbath, Part 2
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: What to Do on the Sabbath (Part 2)- Pastor Tito Lyro.txt
outline: Opening: Remembrance of a departed church member
Continuing the study of what to do on the Lord's Day
The Sabbath as a foretaste of heavenly rest
Conclusion: Embracing the Lord's Day as a covenant gift
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro continues part two of the lesson on what to do on the Sabbath, opening with remembrance of a departed church member. He continues the confessional study of proper Sabbath observance, covering additional applications including works of mercy and the eschatological significance of the Lord's Day.
transcript:
I have been getting a lot of questions from you asking me to give you a list of things you cannot do on the Sabbath. This series is kind of sad in a lot of ways, because it means I didn't do a good job teaching through the material — and that is exactly what I don't want. I want you to use your discernment. I want you to think through your day, and the things you have learned about the Sabbath, and apply them to the way you keep it and make it holy. So a list would not be absolute, because there is the heart element as well. You can spend the whole day in church, reading your Bible, and still not be keeping the Sabbath, because the heart is a big thing. Why you're doing what you're doing is as important as the doing of it. So you have to keep that in mind.

## The Westminster Confession on Duties of Necessity and Mercy

As we think about how we structure our Sunday — our Sabbath day, the Christian Sabbath, the Lord's Day — the confession does give us two categories of exceptions for the Sabbath day. If you grab your hymnal, turn to page 861 using the little numbers on the bottom. There you find chapter 21 of the confession, which is "Of the Sabbath Day." In paragraph 8 — that's what we're considering, just a clause in paragraph 8 — this should be the end of our series on the Sabbath. Look at Confession 21, paragraph 8. It says this:

> *"This Sabbath is then kept holy unto the Lord, when men, after a due preparing of their hearts, and ordering of their common affairs beforehand, do not only observe a holy rest all the day from their own works, words, and thoughts about their worldly employments and recreations, but also are taken up the whole time in the public and private exercises of His worship, and in the duties of necessity and mercy."*

So the confession uses this language of "duties of necessity and mercy." Our minds look at that and we tend to try to fit everything we want to do under those two categories. I think we have to change that way of thinking and actually realize that these are exceptions — these are things you're doing *to* the Sabbath. If there's something that requires you to do it because of necessity or mercy, then you do that as well; but not by figuring out how you can fit everything you want into those two categories so you can just go ahead and do whatever you want.

We have several categories of things for the Lord's Day. Public worship — we considered that; it is the most appropriate and commanded thing for us to do on the Sabbath day. Private worship — another thing that's commanded and appropriate and good to do on the Lord's Day. There are so many things competing for our time on weekdays that this whole day gives us more opportunity to worship the Lord privately, with our families and ourselves, catching up on Bible reading, praying, and so on. Rest — that's another appropriate thing to do on the Sabbath day. The word *Sabbath* literally means rest, so resting is a good thing. Hospitality — I'll use the word in the sense of entertaining people, in the sense of hospitality; Leviticus 23 is a great example of the Sabbath being used for gathering and entertaining people. Ministry in general — going to minister to people in whatever ways you can, visiting people as a whole family. Remember that family time doesn't have to be just sitting in front of the TV. Family time is doing things together as a family, including ministry.

And then we have these two things: duties of mercy and duties of necessity. We have to define what the confession means by these two terms. I think it's not very difficult to define what they mean — it's just whether we want to accept it or not.

## Duties of Mercy and Necessity Tend to Go Together

One thing we have to understand is that duties of necessity and duties of mercy tend to go together. It's very difficult to separate those two categories. Something that we must do out of necessity may also be a duty of mercy. As we look at the Bible, things will become more clearly clear to us. Turn to Matthew 12. I want to read verses 1 through 13 because here Jesus addresses both things — both duties of mercy and duties of necessity.

Matthew 12, starting at verse 1:

> *"At that time Jesus went through the grain fields on the Sabbath, and His disciples were hungry and began to pluck heads of grain and to eat. And when the Pharisees saw it, they said to Him, 'Look, Your disciples are doing what is not lawful to do on the Sabbath!'"* (Matthew 12:1–2)

The Pharisees considered any plucking from any plant as harvesting, and that was forbidden — in *their* book, not in our book called the Bible.

> *"But He said to them, 'Have you not read what David did when he was hungry, he and those who were with him: how he entered the house of God and ate the showbread which was not lawful for him to eat, nor for those who were with him, but only for the priests? Or have you not read in the law that on the Sabbath the priests in the temple profane the Sabbath, and are blameless?'"* (Matthew 12:3–5)

Let's stop here. What is Jesus doing? Is Jesus saying that David broke the law of God? Is He saying the priests broke the law of God? Is that what He's saying? If you just take the text straightforwardly, that's what He's saying — unless you have some other way to explain it. He says that David ate bread that only the priests were to eat, and that the priests would break the Sabbath by doing their work. What is Jesus doing? He is exposing their presuppositions. Their traditions said that if those traditions were true, then David would have broken the law. If their traditions were true, then the priests would break the Sabbath every time they offered sacrifices on the Sabbath. He is adjusting their thinking. They had created something that is not what the Bible teaches. The priests don't break the Sabbath when they act as priests. David didn't break the law when he ate the showbread. But according to the Pharisees' traditions — which superseded the word of God — David had broken the law. He is challenging them here: their traditions have become more important to them than the Bible itself, concerning the Sabbath.

Verse 6:

> *"Yet I say to you that in this place there is One greater than the temple. But if you had known what this means, 'I desire mercy and not sacrifice,' you would not have condemned the guiltless. For the Son of Man is Lord even of the Sabbath."* (Matthew 12:6–8)

That's key. They're condemning somebody who didn't do anything wrong. The priests haven't done anything wrong. David didn't do anything wrong. And the disciples are not doing anything wrong by grabbing grain as they walk through the field to eat on the Sabbath day.

Verse 9:

> *"Now when He had departed from there, He went into their synagogue. And behold, there was a man who had a withered hand. And they asked Him, saying, 'Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath?' — that they might accuse Him."* (Matthew 12:9–10)

So they're trying to catch Him again, asking Him if it's okay to heal on the Sabbath.

> *"Then He said to them, 'What man is there among you who has one sheep, and if it falls into a pit on the Sabbath, will not lay hold of it and lift it out? Of how much more value then is a man than a sheep? Therefore it is lawful to do good on the Sabbath.' Then He healed the man."* (Matthew 12:11–13)

Here He has said that if an ox falls into a ditch, it is necessary to rescue that ox on the Sabbath day. The same way, it is necessary to do good to people on the Sabbath day. And here you see the intermingling of necessity and mercy. It is also merciful to take care of a health emergency on the Sabbath day. It is merciful to have the ER open to help people involved in medical emergencies — and also a matter of necessity. So you see how those things go together.

Any questions on just the reading of this passage? This is just to show that there is such a thing as necessities that should be done on the Sabbath day, and that those necessities tend to mix with mercy as well.

## Mark 3 — The Parallel Passage

Flip over to Mark chapter 3 for a moment. That's the parallel passage to Matthew 12. Verse 1:

> *"And He entered the synagogue again, and a man was there who had a withered hand. So they watched Him closely, whether He would heal him on the Sabbath, so that they might accuse Him. And He said to the man who had the withered hand, 'Step forward.' Then He said to them, 'Is it lawful on the Sabbath to do good or to do evil, to save life or to kill?' But they kept silent."* (Mark 3:1–4)

So you see all the different necessities there. Verse 5:

> *"And when He had looked around at them with anger, being grieved by the hardness of their hearts, He said to the man, 'Stretch out your hand.' And he stretched it out, and his hand was restored as whole as the other."* (Mark 3:5)

You notice there in verse 5 it says He looked at them with anger, being grieved by the hardness of their hearts. We read that and we see Jesus upset at those people who make all those kinds of rules about the Sabbath. But no — He was upset with people who changed the Sabbath in *any* way. He is angry at people who do not consider the Sabbath the way that the Bible teaches it should be kept, whether by being extra rigid — more limiting than the Bible is — or by completely ignoring the Sabbath and what the Lord says. He is angry at both groups here. This is the word Mark uses in Mark chapter 3.

Any questions about the reading of Mark 3, verses 1 through 5?

So I think we can show that Jesus recognized these categories of duties of mercy and necessity that govern what His people are supposed to do on the Sabbath. And importantly, we see also that it is sometimes difficult to separate these two things. It is a necessary thing to take someone who has been in a car accident to the emergency room on the Sabbath — and it is also an act of mercy to do that for them. Keeping a hospital staffed is an act of necessity and an act of mercy. Having first responders ready to go is an act of necessity and of mercy — though perhaps there could be better systems to manage that. Staffing a power plant is an act of necessity and mercy as well. Soldiers in a war zone — that's an act of necessity, though I'm not as sure about soldiers stateside being on duty on the Lord's Day. A police force operating in the city is a matter of necessity. Taking care of our animals on the Sabbath is a work of necessity. So you can see these different categories, and we can use our minds to extrapolate and apply them to our situation.

## What Does Not Qualify as a Necessity

It's important to think about this too: a true work of necessity on the Sabbath is not something that could have been done some other day but we didn't get to it. For example, if you're a student and you have something due that was assigned ten days ago but you didn't take care of it on any other day, and now you think you'll do it on the Sabbath because it's due tomorrow — that is not a genuine necessity. The ox is not in the ditch just because you are behind in your work. It is not a work of necessity to finish something that you could have finished during the other six days and didn't, and now you are in a bind because it's due.

Any questions before we continue?

*[Jonas asks about the situation of not being diligent and waiting until Sunday to do work due on Monday — is it better to skip the work and take the failing grade, or to do the work and repent?]*

Let me ask you this: should we sin to solve sin? No. So the answer is no — we do not resolve sin with more sin. Why is it that you won't take the F? Because that might dishonor you. Why is it that you are going to do the work on Sunday even though it might be dishonorable? Because sometimes we are more worried about our own honor and the consequences that come upon us than we are about the honor of the Lord and exalting Him.

*[Katie restates the question: the student doesn't do their homework, it's Sunday night, and if they don't finish they'll get an F tomorrow — do they go ahead and do the homework?]*

My question back is: do we sin in order to solve sin? The answer is no.

*[Amy asks about working from home where the only time she can print something is on Sunday, otherwise she might lose housing.]*

Well, there's not just "this or that" — there are all kinds of things in between. We are also making a false argument by saying it's either work on Sunday or be homeless. There are all kinds of different things that could be done. And at the end of the day, if being faithful to the Lord results in hardship, that has been the case for Christians for centuries. Our Lord Himself didn't always have a place to sleep — He said the foxes have holes and the birds of the air have nests, but the Son of Man had nowhere to lay His head.

*[Katie asks about what would constitute a true emergency, such as running out of food.]*

If you're truly out of food — you open the fridge on Monday, there's food; Tuesday, there's food; Wednesday, there's food; Thursday, there's food; Friday, there's food; Saturday, there's food; then Sunday, somehow there's nothing and you haven't eaten in five days and you're going to die — yes, those situations exist. But you don't find them very often.

*[Lisa asks a question.]*

*[A different voice responds: isn't that exactly the Pharisees' argument — "come back tomorrow to be healed"?]*

But was Jesus there tomorrow? Jesus was passing through the area — He didn't stay in the same place all the time. That was the moment Jesus was there. For all we know, that man was never going to see Jesus again.

*[Tim asks whether there is a personal element of individual conviction in these matters.]*

There is a small element of that, yes — in that if you're not convinced that something is right to do, even if it isn't actually wrong, you shouldn't do it. You shouldn't go against your conscience. But I think this is way more objective than we want it to be. We want the Sabbath to be completely subjective — just up to me — and that ends up meaning almost anything I want. I do think it is far more objective than we would like it to be.

## Our Responsibility for Those in Our Sphere of Influence

One thing that we forget is that we are responsible not only for ourselves but also for those in our sphere of influence. Grab your Bibles and turn to Exodus chapter 20. Look at verse 8, and I'll read through verse 11:

> *"Remember the Sabbath day, to keep it holy. Six days you shall labor and do all your work, but the seventh day is the Sabbath of the Lord your God. In it you shall do no work: you, nor your son, nor your daughter, nor your male servant, nor your female servant, nor your cattle, nor your stranger who is within your gates. For in six days the Lord made the heavens and the earth, the sea, and all that is in them, and rested the seventh day. Therefore the Lord blessed the Sabbath day and hallowed it."* (Exodus 20:8–11)

Notice — it's not just us. We are also responsible for other people. We shouldn't put people in a position where they have to break the Sabbath because of us. We are not to put them in a position where they have to work in worldly employment because of us. And so the question about the grocery store — that's exactly what we're doing when we shop on Sunday. We're putting other people in the position of having to work in order to serve us, because we either wanted something or we didn't plan ahead. The argument "well, the store is going to be open whether I go there or not" doesn't matter. You're not called to figure out what the store is going to do. You're called to figure out what *you* should do in obedience to the Lord. The same applies to restaurants, gyms, and retail. Retail is not an emergency. The mall is not an emergency. These are things we need to think about: am I causing other people to work so that I can do this, when I don't need to be doing this at this time? Heads of households are responsible for all that is going on in their household. Because we love God and we love others, we don't want to put people in a position where they have to sin in order to serve us — because we love them too much to do that to them.

*[Katie asks whether that means one cannot go to a favorite restaurant on Sunday.]*

When we talked about recreation — there is a proper way to use the Sabbath to recreate with family, with church people, and so on. There are all kinds of ways to do that that don't necessarily cause that problem. Just think it through: can I do this in a way that is glorifying to God? In being together, are we blessing the Lord, blessing our family — and not being complicit in someone having to work because I'm there? Think through it and apply that.

## Closing Review

Any other questions? All right. So a closing review of what we covered during these weeks and months:

We saw from chapter 21 that creation and our own nature tells us there is a God who must be worshiped — that is clear from creation itself. We also saw that the way of worshiping this God is only disclosed in the Bible, and that without the Bible we really don't know how to worship Him. We saw that worship is always Trinitarian — directed to God the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. We saw that there are several elements in private and corporate worship: reading the Bible, preaching the Bible, hearing the Bible, singing the Bible, eating the Word in the sacraments. Private worship also includes many of these things. We saw that nature itself shows us that there is a Sabbath, and that the Sabbath was the last day of the week but is now the first day of the week because of the resurrection of Christ, and that it is a whole day of worship — public and private. Those are the things we covered in the last two months of studying the Sabbath.

Any questions as we finish our series on the Sabbath?
